hero.title: 6 Days, Arupadai Veedu.
hero.titleAccent: The Six Sacred Abodes of Lord Murugan.
hero.intro: One of the most revered pilgrimages in Tamil tradition. The Arupadai Veedu, the six holy abodes of Lord Murugan, scattered across Tamil Nadu's hills and coasts. Six days of devotion through some of the south's most sacred temples.
hero.heroImageAlt: Murugan temple Arupadai Veedu Tamil Nadu

overview.heading: 6 Days Arupadai Veedu Murugan Temples: The Full Picture
body: The Arupadai Veedu are the six sacred abodes of Lord Murugan, the Tamil god of war and victory, son of Shiva and Parvati. Spread across Tamil Nadu from hilltops to the coast, these temples form one of the most important pilgrimages in Tamil Shaivite tradition, drawing devotees from across the world.

This 6 day Arupadai Veedu package from Malaysia is arranged privately, with a driver guide who understands the routing, rituals and significance of each abode. The pilgrimage covers Palani, Swamimalai, Thiruchendur, Thirupparamkundram, Pazhamudircholai and Thiruthani, taking in the sacred geography of Murugan worship.

highlights.heading: Why Travellers from Malaysia Love This Trip

items.title: The Six Abodes of Murugan
items.body: Palani, Swamimalai, Thiruchendur, Thirupparamkundram, Pazhamudircholai and Thiruthani, each with its own legend, location and significance in the Murugan tradition.

items.title: Hilltop and Coastal Temples
items.body: From the hill temple of Palani to the seaside shrine of Thiruchendur on the Bay of Bengal, the abodes span dramatic and varied sacred settings.

items.title: Living Tamil Devotion
items.body: These are vibrant, living temples, full of ritual, music and devotion. A guide brings the legends and customs of Murugan worship to life at each stop.

days.title: Madurai to Thiruchendur

sections.heading: Arupadai Veedu Tour from Malaysia
sections.body:
The Arupadai Veedu pilgrimage is one of the most significant in Tamil Shaivite tradition, visiting the six sacred abodes of Lord Murugan across Tamil Nadu.

This private 6 day package handles the considerable routing between the temples and pairs you with a guide who understands the legends and rituals, making the pilgrimage smooth and meaningful.

For an even fuller temple journey, our 7 day Arupadai Veedu plus Navagraha package combines the Murugan abodes with the nine planetary temples.

sections.heading: Who is Lord Murugan?
sections.body:
Lord Murugan, also known as Kartikeya or Subramanya, is the Tamil god of war and victory, the son of Shiva and Parvati and brother of Ganesha. He is especially revered in Tamil Nadu and among the Tamil diaspora, including in Malaysia.

The six abodes, the Arupadai Veedu, each commemorate an episode in Murugan's mythology, making the pilgrimage a journey through his sacred story.

items.question: Do Malaysians need a visa for India?
items.answer: Yes. Malaysian passport holders apply for an India e-Visa online before departure. The tourist e-Visa covers tourism and short visits and is valid for 30 days from entry. The application is on the official India e-Visa portal, and Dhesu walks you through it when you book. Visa fees are not included in the package price.

items.question: Which airport does this trip use?
items.answer: This is a land-only package: hotels, transfers, tours and guide are included, but international flights are not. Most Malaysian travellers fly from Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) to Chennai or Madurai Airport (MAA / IXM). We can recommend suitable flights and timings on request.

items.question: What is the best time for the Arupadai Veedu pilgrimage?
items.answer: November to February is the most comfortable, avoiding the intense Tamil Nadu heat. Festivals like Thaipusam and Skanda Sashti draw the largest crowds at Murugan temples, so book early if you wish to visit during these.

items.question: Are the six temples far apart?
items.answer: Yes, the abodes are spread across Tamil Nadu, from hilltops to the coast, so the pilgrimage involves significant driving. The 6 day pace and private vehicle make this comfortable, with the route planned to be as efficient as possible.

items.question: Is there a dress code?
items.answer: Yes. Traditional, modest dress is expected at these temples. Men often wear dhoti and women a saree or salwar kameez for darshan. You remove footwear before entering. Your guide will advise on each temple's specific customs.
